Significant Details: Governing body for professional surfers, created by IPS in 1976, ASP took over in 1983, rebranded as WSL in 2015, Sophie Goldschmidt appointed CEO in 2017
Significant Changes/Milestones: Acquisition by ZoSea in 2013, Sophie Goldschmidt appointed CEO in 2017, exclusive digital broadcast deal with Facebook in 2018

Significant Details: National competition finals held in Newcastle, New South Wales since 2017, Fox Sports airs the event live, national finals of first three series held at Cronulla Beach in Sydney on Australia Day weekend
Notable Changes/Cancellations: National finals location changed to Newcastle in 2017

Notable Competitors: Men and women surf athletes from around the world
Significant Details: Annual surf, skate, and music event, World Surf League sanctioned, inaugural event held in February 2012, event replaced the 'Malfunction' Surf Festival in 1984, largest WSL sanctioned longboard competition in Australia, held in March at Kingscliff, Tweed Heads, NSW
Notable Changes/Cancellations: Event cancelled in 2018, not run in 2019 and 2020

Significant Details: Held since 1961, sponsored by Rip Curl since 1973, iconic 'Bell' trophy, "Hells Bells" by AC/DC played, cancelled in 2020 due to COVID-19, trophies presented by Doug 'Claw' Warbrick

Notable Competitors: Top 34 surfers from WSL, 32 walk-on trial surfers
Significant Details: Annual event, sponsored by Billabong since 2007, 2020 event canceled due to COVID-19, 2020 Pipeline Masters served as opening event of 2020-21 season
Duration: Final leg of Triple Crown of Surfing, final event on WSL Championship Tour

Notable Competitors: Duke Paoa Kahinu Mokoe Hulikohola Kahanamoku, Greg Noll, Fred Hemmings, Jeff Hakman, Clyde Aikau, Eddie Aikau
Significant Details: First surfing event broadcast on ABC's Wide World of Sports, winners received golden "Duke" statues, notable big-wave surfers, replaced by Billabong Pro in 1985
Location: Sunset Beach, North Shore of Oʻahu
Notable Changes/Cancellations: Replaced by Billabong Pro in 1985, Duke Kahanamoku's death in 1968

Significant Details: Longest running surfing contest in the world, 58th annual competition held in 2020 with safety guidelines, includes men's and women's events, various beach sporting events, Beach Bum Classic triathlon, concerts on the beach
Notable Changes/Cancellations: 2020 event held amid the coronavirus pandemic with safety guidelines

Notable Competitors: Aaron Napoleon, Bonga Perkins, Jamie Sterling, Garrett McNamara, Raimana Van Bastolaer, Buzzy Kerbox, Chris Mauro, Kainoa McGee
Significant Details: Annual big-wave competition, first held on February 14, 2008, surf between 15 and 25 feet, organized by Brian Keaulana

Established in 1992 as Noosa Malibu Classic, introduced professional division in 1996, renamed to Noosa Festival of Surfing in 1998, World Tandem Surfing Championships in 1999, Stand up paddle surfing added in 2007, ASL Longboard rated event, sponsored by Global Surf Industries, managed by USM Events
Significant Changes/Milestones: Mixing of the Waters ceremony led by Brian Keaulana, Heart of Australian surfing in the late 50s and early 60s, Kevin Platt's influence on surfboard design, Trevor Hewston's innovations, perfect waves at Noosa

Significant Details: First bodysurfing event at Pipeline, goals based on North Shore Expressive/Paipo bodysurfing event, open to men and women, adopted by North Shore Bodysurfing Club, denied permits in certain seasons, exclusive access to Pipeline
Notable Changes/Cancellations: Denied contest permits for multiple seasons

Notable Competitors: Eddie Aikau, top big wave surfers
Significant Details: Big wave surfing tournament named after Eddie Aikau, requires 60-foot wave faces, held only nine times due to specific wave height criteria
Surfing competition in the World Surf League, held at Coolangatta, Queensland, Australia, event canceled in August 2021

Notable Competitors: Grant "Twiggy" Baker, competitors from the United States, Hawaii, Brazil, and South Africa
Significant Details: Annual competition held at "Dungeons" near The Sentinel, known for large Atlantic swells, 24 competitors in 2008, won by Grant "Twiggy" Baker
Location: Cape Town, Western Cape, South Africa

Notable Competitors: Richie Vaculik, Evan Faulks, Mark Mathews, Ryan Hipwood, Dion Thomson
Significant Details: Big wave surfing competition held at Cape Fear, also known as 'Ours', organized by Red Bull, Dion Thomson emerged as the winner

Notable Competitors: Nathan Purcell, Mark Fordham, Mike Stewart, Guilherme Tamega
Significant Details: Premier World Tour event in bodyboarding, informal start in 1997, international event since 2002, replaced Teahupoo Challenge, relaxed atmosphere in early years

Sponsored by Coca-Cola and 2SM Radio, held in Sydney from 1974 to 1991, televised on Channel 9, winner received a Gold Logie for 'Best Sports Event', mobile contest with a waiting period, man on man format, moved to Bells Beach in 1979 due to lack of waves in Sydney
Notable Changes/Cancellations: Last held in 1991, winner of a "pick the best wave" competition got a spot in the event

Significant Details: Annual surfing competition, initiated by Newcastle City Council, world's richest surfing competition in 1985, held at Merewether Beach since 2007, festival-style approach since 2015
Largest surfing competition in the world, WSL QS 10,000 event, Surfing Walk of Fame, Surfers' Hall of Fame, $21.5 million economic impact
